title: Library headers and declarations

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A project header declares int total(const int *values, size_t count) and each caller includes it while one .c file defines the function. Your goal is to select required headers and explain the danger of missing or incompatible declarations. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Translation unit and main. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A header exposes declarations, types, and macros shared by translation units, while non-inline external definitions normally belong in exactly one source file. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Header inclusion is textual, macro state and order matter, and incorrect tentative or external definitions can create duplicate symbols or inconsistent declarations. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Including a header automatically links a library implementation and makes every declared function definition available.

includes: Include the header that declares each standard-library facility and respect its contract.
Select required headers and explain the danger of missing or incompatible declarations.
excludes: Header inclusion is textual, macro state and order matter, and incorrect tentative or external definitions can create duplicate symbols or inconsistent declarations.

outcomes: Select required headers and explain the danger of missing or incompatible declarations.

examples.title: Library headers and declarations: defined case
examples.situation: A project header declares int total(const int *values, size_t count) and each caller includes it while one .c file defines the function.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A project header declares int total(const int *values, size_t count) and each caller includes it while one .c file defines the function.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A header supplies compile-time declarations; the matching object or library definition must also be linked, and declarations must remain compatible.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A header supplies compile-time declarations; the matching object or library definition must also be linked, and declarations must remain compatible.

examples.title: Library headers and declarations: boundary case
examples.situation: An include guard prevents repeated declarations when two headers include the same shared type header through different paths.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: An include guard prevents repeated declarations when two headers include the same shared type header through different paths.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Header inclusion is textual, macro state and order matter, and incorrect tentative or external definitions can create duplicate symbols or inconsistent declarations.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Including a header automatically links a library implementation and makes every declared function definition available.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Select required headers and explain the danger of missing or incompatible declarations.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Including a header automatically links a library implementation and makes every declared function definition available.
misconceptions.correction: A header supplies compile-time declarations; the matching object or library definition must also be linked, and declarations must remain compatible.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Including a header automatically links a library implementation and makes every declared function definition available.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Library headers and declarations: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Header inclusion is textual, macro state and order matter, and incorrect tentative or external definitions can create duplicate symbols or inconsistent declarations.
